table_of_contents.title: Recap: classification, kNN, linear classifiers, and data loss
Regularization: intuition, penalties, and weight preferences
Optimization setup: loss landscapes, random search, and gradients
Gradient descent and stochastic minibatch training
Adaptive optimizers: RMSProp, Adam, and AdamW
Learning-rate schedules, second-order optimization, and neural-network preview

units.name: Recap: image classification, kNN, linear scores, and data loss
units.description: Connects image classification and linear classifiers to the loss formulation used later for regularization and optimization.
units.summary: Image classification is framed as mapping an image to one label from a fixed set such as cat, dog, bird, deer, or truck [ts=27s]. The recap emphasizes the semantic gap between human perception and pixel tensors, plus viewpoint, illumination, deformation, occlusion, clutter, and intraclass variation [ts=64s]. Because hand-written rules are inadequate, kNN provides a data-driven baseline: 1-NN copies the closest training label, and k-NN uses the most common label among nearby examples [ts=182s]. Validation data is used to choose hyperparameters such as k, while the test set is saved for final unseen-data evaluation [ts=216s]. Linear classifiers compute class scores by flattening a 32x32x3 image into 3,072 values and multiplying by W plus bias [ts=558s]. A loss function formalizes how bad the classifier is: dataset loss averages per-example losses, and Softmax/cross-entropy is high when the correct class has low predicted probability [ts=626s].

key_points.text: Image classification maps an input image to a label from a fixed label set.
Recognition is difficult because pixels differ from semantic human perception and because images vary by viewpoint, illumination, deformation, occlusion, clutter, and intraclass variation.
In kNN, 1-NN assigns the closest training label; larger k assigns the most common label among neighbors.
Use validation data to choose hyperparameters and reserve the test set for final unseen-data evaluation.
A 32x32 RGB image has 32*32*3 = 3,072 values for a linear classifier.
Dataset loss is the average of per-example losses.

units.name: Regularization: intuition, penalties, and weight preferences
units.description: Introduces regularization as the second loss term and explains why L1 and L2 encode different solution preferences.
units.summary: Regularization is introduced as a term that prevents the model from doing too well on the training data, intentionally trading worse training fit for better test or unseen-data performance [ts=714s]. A toy example contrasts f1, which fits every training point, with f2, which does not fit perfectly but is expected to generalize better [ts=787s]. This is connected to Occam's Razor: among competing hypotheses, prefer the simpler one first [ts=854s]. The lambda parameter is the regularization-strength hyperparameter; lambda = 0 gives no regularization and larger values strengthen the penalty [ts=882s]. L2 squares and sums weights, L1 sums absolute values, and elastic net combines L1 and L2 [ts=923s]. L2 tends to prefer many small nonzero weights, while L1 tends to push dispensable weights to zero [ts=976s]. In the W1/W2 class example, W2=[0.25,0.25,0.25,0.25] and W1=[1,0,0,0] give the same score, but L2 prefers W2 because its squared penalty is lower [ts=1185s]. For the same example, L1 gives both weights equal penalty even though L1 often encourages sparsity in practice [ts=1241s].

key_points.text: Regularization is added to data loss to reduce overfitting: worse training performance can improve test performance.
A simpler function that does not fit every training point can generalize better than a function that fits all training data perfectly.
Lambda controls regularization strength and is tuned as a hyperparameter.
L2 regularization squares and sums weights; L1 regularization sums absolute weight values.
L2 prefers spread-out small weights; L1 tends to encourage sparsity.
For W1=[1,0,0,0] and W2=[0.25,0.25,0.25,0.25], L2 prefers W2, while L1 gives equal penalty in this exact setup.

units.name: Optimization setup: loss landscapes, random search, and gradients
units.description: Frames optimization as finding the best W in a loss landscape and contrasts random search with gradient-based search.
units.summary: After defining data loss plus regularization, the lecture asks how to find the best W; this is the optimization problem [ts=1474s]. The loss-landscape analogy treats vertical position as loss and horizontal position as model parameters; the optimizer is like a blindfolded person who can only feel local slope [ts=1488s]. Random search is shown as a deliberately bad first idea: trying 1,000 random W values gives about 15.5% CIFAR-10 accuracy, far below modern roughly 99.7% accuracy [ts=1567s]. The better strategy is to follow the slope: in one dimension this is the derivative, and in multiple dimensions the gradient is the vector of partial derivatives [ts=1654s]. The slope in a direction is the dot product of the direction with the gradient, and steepest descent is the negative gradient [ts=1716s]. Numerical gradients perturb one coordinate by a small h, recompute loss, subtract, and divide by h; this is easy but slow and approximate [ts=1729s]. Analytic gradients use calculus and the chain rule to compute dW exactly and quickly, but they can be implementation-error-prone, so numerical gradients are used for gradient checking [ts=1883s].

key_points.text: Optimization asks how to find the best W once scores, data loss, and regularization are defined.
A loss landscape maps model parameters to loss, and the optimizer only gets local slope information.
Random search over W achieved about 15.5% CIFAR-10 accuracy, which is much worse than modern state of the art.
In multiple dimensions, the gradient is the vector of partial derivatives.
The steepest descent direction is the negative gradient.
Numerical gradients use finite differences such as (new_loss - old_loss)/h, but are slow and approximate.
Use analytic gradients in practice, but compare them to numerical gradients for gradient checking.

units.name: Gradient descent and stochastic minibatch training
units.description: Turns gradients into iterative optimization algorithms and explains why minibatches lead to SGD.
units.summary: The lecture clarifies that if a loss is non-differentiable across most of its domain, steepest-descent methods may not give meaningful directions toward a solution [ts=1953s]. Gradient descent computes gradients of W from the loss, data, and current weights, then updates weights by moving in the negative-gradient direction scaled by a learning rate or step size [ts=2074s]. A contour plot shows original W, the negative-gradient direction, and a fixed step-size arrow moving toward lower loss [ts=2098s]. Stopping can be based on a fixed number of iterations or a tolerance when the loss no longer decreases enough [ts=2145s]. Full gradient descent requires summing over the entire training set, which becomes expensive when N is large [ts=2195s]. Stochastic gradient descent approximates the full sum using a randomly sampled minibatch, such as 256 training examples, then applies the same update rule [ts=2215s]. The randomness of the minibatch is what makes the method stochastic [ts=2232s].

key_points.text: If a loss is largely non-differentiable, steepest descent may not point toward a useful solution.
Gradient descent repeatedly computes gradients and moves in the negative-gradient direction.
The update subtracts a learning-rate-scaled gradient from the current weights.
Training can stop after fixed iterations or when loss improvement falls below a tolerance.
SGD approximates the full dataset gradient using a randomly sampled minibatch.
A batch size of 256 means each step estimates the gradient from 256 examples.

units.name: SGD failure modes and momentum
units.description: Explains vanilla SGD pathologies and how momentum uses velocity to address them.
units.summary: Vanilla SGD can struggle when the loss changes quickly in one direction and slowly in another: it may overshoot or jitter along the steep direction while making slow progress along the shallow direction [ts=2318s]. This poor conditioning is connected to a large ratio between the Hessian's largest and smallest singular values [ts=2385s]. Another problem is local minima or saddle points, where zero or tiny gradients can stop gradient descent even though lower loss may be nearby [ts=2406s]. Saddle points become more common in high-dimensional models [ts=2487s]. A third problem is minibatch noise, because each SGD update estimates gradients from only a random subset [ts=2559s]. Momentum addresses these problems by building a velocity like a ball rolling down a hill, so previous gradients influence the next step and can smooth noise, pass through flat/saddle regions, and reduce oscillation [ts=2586s]. In the equations, SGD updates directly from the current gradient, while SGD+Momentum updates by velocity; rho controls how much past velocity is retained, often 0.9 or 0.99 [ts=2679s].

key_points.text: Poor conditioning makes SGD jitter in steep directions and progress slowly in shallow directions.
A too-large learning rate can overshoot and oscillate out of the valley.
Local minima and saddle points can have zero gradients, causing gradient descent to get stuck.
Saddle points are empirically more common in high-dimensional models.
Minibatch gradients are noisy because each update uses only a sampled subset of data.
Momentum builds a velocity or running average of gradients across iterations.
Momentum uses rho to weight past velocity versus current slope; typical rho values are 0.9 or 0.99.

units.name: Adaptive optimizers: RMSProp, Adam, and AdamW
units.description: Introduces adaptive per-parameter scaling, Adam's combination of momentum and RMSProp, and decoupled weight decay in AdamW.
units.summary: RMSProp is introduced as an adaptive optimizer that changes update scale by dividing by a decayed squared-gradient term [ts=2969s]. Its intuition is to take smaller steps in directions with large squared gradients and larger steps in flatter directions, so steep directions are damped and flat directions accelerated [ts=3039s]. The visual comparison shows RMSProp turning toward the valley center more quickly than SGD because it changes the relative step sizes by dimension [ts=3159s]. Adam is described as roughly RMSProp with momentum: it combines a first-moment velocity with a second-moment squared-gradient estimate [ts=3226s]. A naive Adam form has a first-timestep issue because moment estimates start at zero and beta values such as 0.9 and 0.999 are close to 1, so the full Adam form adds bias correction [ts=3305s]. Adam with beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, and learning rate 1e-3 or 5e-4 is recommended as a strong starting point for many models [ts=3403s]. AdamW decouples weight decay: standard Adam includes L2 in moment calculations, while AdamW applies decay after computing moments so velocity reflects the loss landscape more independently of raw weight values [ts=3457s].

key_points.text: RMSProp adds element-wise scaling based on historical squared gradients with decay.
RMSProp dampens steep directions and accelerates flatter directions.
Adam combines momentum-style first moments with RMSProp-style second moments.
Adam needs bias correction because moment estimates start at zero.
A common Adam starting point is beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, and learning rate 1e-3 or 5e-4.
Standard Adam includes L2 in moment calculations, while AdamW applies weight decay after moments are computed.

units.name: Learning-rate schedules, second-order optimization, and the neural-network transition
units.description: Covers learning-rate tuning and schedules, Hessian-based methods, practical optimizer defaults, and the preview of neural networks.
units.summary: All discussed optimizers have learning rate as a hyperparameter [ts=3525s]. A very high learning rate can make loss explode by oscillating out of the landscape, a very low learning rate converges slowly, and a still-too-high rate near a minimum can keep the optimizer bouncing around [ts=3556s]. Modern deep learning often varies learning rate during training [ts=3609s]. Step decay reduces the learning rate at fixed points, such as multiplying by 0.1 during ResNet training [ts=3625s]. Cosine decay follows a half-cosine from maximum learning rate toward zero; other options include linear and inverse-square-root decay [ts=3654s]. Linear warmup ramps from zero to the maximum learning rate before another scheduler, and a common empirical rule is to scale learning rate by N when batch size increases by N [ts=3726s]. Second-order optimization uses the Hessian to form a quadratic approximation instead of only a gradient-based linear approximation, but Hessian methods are rarely used for large neural networks because second derivatives and Hessian matrices are too expensive in memory and computation [ts=3819s]. The practical advice is that Adam or AdamW is a strong default, AdamW with warmup and cosine decay is common, SGD+Momentum can outperform Adam with more tuning, and small full-batch problems may justify second-order methods [ts=4006s]. The lecture closes by previewing neural networks: a 2-layer network uses two weight matrices with a nonlinearity such as ReLU between them, enabling transformations that can make non-linearly separable points separable by a final linear classifier [ts=4044s].

key_points.text: Learning rate is a hyperparameter for SGD, SGD+Momentum, RMSProp, Adam, and AdamW.
Too-high learning rates can explode or bounce around minima; too-low learning rates converge slowly.
Step decay reduces learning rate at fixed points, such as multiplying by 0.1 during ResNet training.
Cosine decay follows a half-cosine schedule from maximum learning rate toward zero.
Linear warmup ramps learning rate from zero to a target maximum before continuing with a scheduler.
Linear scaling rule: if batch size increases by N, increase learning rate by N.
Second-order optimization uses the Hessian to build a quadratic approximation but is impractical for very large deep networks.
A 2-layer neural network inserts a nonlinearity such as ReLU between two weight matrices.

concepts_kp_local.name: Image classification and recognition challenges
concepts_kp_local.description: Assigning an image to a label despite semantic gap, viewpoint, illumination, deformation, occlusion, clutter, and intraclass variation.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Image classification is described as a core computer-vision task and motivates data-driven methods [ts=27s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=27s] show a cat image mapped to class labels and separate recognition challenge examples.

concepts_kp_local.name: Data-driven kNN and validation/test splits
concepts_kp_local.description: Using nearest training examples to classify and using validation data to select hyperparameters while preserving test data for final evaluation.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: kNN and validation are reviewed as the simplest data-driven baseline and hyperparameter-selection setup [ts=216s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=216s], the slide shows train/validation/test splits and kNN validation accuracy curves.

concepts_kp_local.name: Linear classifier scores and loss functions
concepts_kp_local.description: Linear score functions, Softmax/cross-entropy, and dataset loss as an average of per-example losses.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Regularization and optimization both require a score function and differentiable loss over W [ts=626s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=590s] show class scores for cat/car/frog and the dataset loss formula.

concepts_kp_local.name: Regularization and generalization
concepts_kp_local.description: Adding a penalty to reduce overfitting and improve unseen-data performance.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Regularization is one of the two title topics and is explicitly defined as worsening training fit to improve test performance [ts=714s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=787s], the toy curve slide contrasts perfect training fit with a simpler generalizing model.

concepts_kp_local.name: L1, L2, elastic net, and lambda
concepts_kp_local.description: Common regularization penalties and the lambda hyperparameter controlling penalty strength.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The lecture lists L2, L1, elastic net, and lambda as concrete regularization mechanisms [ts=923s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: The slide at [ts=923s] lists the L2, L1, and elastic-net formulas.

concepts_kp_local.name: Weight-preference effects of regularization
concepts_kp_local.description: Regularizers encode preferences such as L2 spread-out weights and L1 sparsity.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The W1/W2 activity demonstrates that different penalties select different equally scoring solutions [ts=1185s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=1185s], the slide displays W1 and W2 with equal scores and asks which regularizer prefers which weights.

concepts_kp_local.name: Loss landscapes and optimization objective
concepts_kp_local.description: Viewing loss as a landscape over parameters, where training searches for low-loss settings.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The loss-landscape analogy bridges loss definitions to gradient-based optimizer behavior [ts=1488s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=1488s] show a landscape/hiker analogy for searching over W.

concepts_kp_local.name: Numerical gradients, analytic gradients, and gradient checking
concepts_kp_local.description: Finite-difference derivative approximations, exact calculus-based gradients, and implementation checks comparing the two.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Analytic gradients are the practical foundation for training, while gradient checking is a key implementation diagnostic [ts=1883s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides at [ts=1729s] show finite-difference calculations such as (1.25322 - 1.25347)/0.0001.

concepts_kp_local.name: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent
concepts_kp_local.description: Iteratively updating weights by negative gradients, using minibatches for scalable stochastic estimates.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Gradient descent is the base optimization algorithm, and SGD is presented as its most popular practical variant [ts=2215s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=2098s], the contour plot shows original W, negative-gradient direction, and a step-size arrow.

concepts_kp_local.name: SGD failure modes
concepts_kp_local.description: Poor conditioning, overshoot, local minima, saddle points, and minibatch gradient noise.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: These pathologies motivate momentum, RMSProp, Adam, and learning-rate schedules [ts=2318s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides from [ts=2318s] to [ts=2559s] illustrate elongated valleys, saddles, and noisy minibatch gradients.

concepts_kp_local.name: Momentum optimizer
concepts_kp_local.description: Using velocity or a running average of gradients to smooth updates and keep moving in useful directions.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Momentum is the first optimizer refinement after SGD problems and later becomes part of Adam [ts=2586s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=2679s], slides compare SGD and SGD+Momentum update equations.

concepts_kp_local.name: RMSProp and adaptive learning rates
concepts_kp_local.description: Element-wise update scaling by historical squared gradients, giving per-parameter adaptive learning rates.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: RMSProp introduces the adaptive scaling idea later combined with momentum in Adam [ts=3039s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=3159s], the slide animation compares SGD, SGD+Momentum, and RMSProp trajectories.

concepts_kp_local.name: Adam and AdamW
concepts_kp_local.description: Adam combines momentum and RMSProp-style adaptivity; AdamW decouples weight decay from moment estimation.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Adam/AdamW are recommended as default optimizers and are broadly used across modern deep learning [ts=4006s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3226s] show Adam moment equations, bias correction, and AdamW weight-decay placement.

concepts_kp_local.name: Learning-rate schedules, warmup, and batch-size scaling
concepts_kp_local.description: Changing learning rates during training using step, cosine, linear, inverse-square-root schedules, warmup, and linear scaling with batch size.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: The instructor says modern models commonly vary learning rate during training and gives practical schedule recipes [ts=3609s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: Slides around [ts=3654s] plot step, cosine, linear, inverse-square-root, and warmup schedules.

concepts_kp_local.name: Second-order optimization and Hessian limitations
concepts_kp_local.description: Using Hessians and quadratic approximations, and why these methods are usually impractical for large deep networks.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: Second-order methods are useful context and may work for small/full-batch problems, but are rarely used for large neural networks because of memory and compute [ts=3886s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=3819s], slides contrast first-order linear approximations with second-order quadratic approximations.

concepts_kp_local.name: Neural-network nonlinearity preview
concepts_kp_local.description: A two-layer neural network adds a nonlinearity between weight matrices to transform data into a linearly separable representation.
concepts_kp_local.importance_rationale: This is a preview of the next lecture and unlocks neural networks and backpropagation, but it is only briefly introduced here [ts=4044s].
concepts_kp_local.visual_evidence: At [ts=4044s], slides show a two-layer network with a ReLU-like nonlinearity and non-linearly separable red/blue points.

unit_kp_map_local.coverage_rationale: The recap defines image classification and lists recognition challenges [ts=27s].
kNN and train/validation/test splits are reviewed as the data-driven baseline and hyperparameter setup [ts=216s].
Linear classifiers, class scores, Softmax/cross-entropy, and averaged dataset loss are reviewed as setup for the new content [ts=626s].
The regularization term is introduced as the second component after data loss [ts=714s].
Regularization is defined as preventing too-good training fit to improve unseen-data performance [ts=714s].
Lambda, L2, L1, and elastic net are introduced as concrete regularization terms [ts=882s].
The W1/W2 examples show how L2 and L1 express different preferences over weights [ts=1185s].
Optimization is introduced as finding the best W on a loss landscape [ts=1474s].
The unit contrasts finite-difference numerical gradients with analytic gradients and gradient checks [ts=1883s].
The differentiability discussion continues from gradient computation [ts=1953s].
Gradient descent and SGD are defined through negative-gradient and minibatch updates [ts=2074s].
The SGD problems arise from the minibatch gradient setting introduced just before this section [ts=2559s].
The unit enumerates poor conditioning, local minima/saddle points, and noisy gradients as SGD problems [ts=2318s].
Momentum is introduced as velocity that carries update direction across iterations [ts=2586s].
Adam is motivated as combining RMSProp with momentum [ts=3226s].
RMSProp is introduced as adaptive per-parameter scaling using squared gradients [ts=2969s].
Adam, bias correction, default hyperparameters, and AdamW decoupled weight decay occupy the central part of the unit [ts=3226s].
The practical optimizer recommendation names Adam/AdamW as strong defaults [ts=4006s].
The unit teaches learning-rate failure modes, schedules, warmup, and batch-size scaling [ts=3525s].
Second-order optimization is introduced through Hessians and limited by memory/computation for deep networks [ts=3819s].
The final preview introduces 2-layer neural networks, ReLU-style nonlinearity, and why nonlinearity helps separability [ts=4044s].

section_flags.rationale: This is recap material establishing image classification, kNN, linear scores, and data loss; formulas are present but mostly reviewed [ts=626s].
Regularization is a title topic, with lambda, L1, L2, elastic net, and W1/W2 penalty examples [ts=923s].
The section shows finite-difference gradient calculations and random-search code snippets [ts=1729s].
Gradient descent and SGD are central algorithms with update equations and minibatch pseudocode [ts=2074s].
SGD pathologies and momentum are highly testable; slides show equations and trajectory animations [ts=2679s].
RMSProp, Adam, AdamW, moments, bias correction, and weight decay placement are formula-heavy optimizer material [ts=3226s].
Learning-rate schedules, warmup, Hessian methods, and optimizer defaults are high-value review topics with formulas and diagnostic rules [ts=3654s].
